summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the definition of 'Two-way table (contingency table)'?

"Two-way table (contingency table)" means: A table displaying counts for every combination of two categorical variables, with row and column totals (margins). The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Marginal distribution'?

"Marginal distribution" means: The distribution of one categorical variable alone, found in the row or column totals of a two-way table. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Joint distribution'?

"Joint distribution" means: The distribution of counts for every combination of categories in a two-way table, expressed as proportions of the grand total. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Conditional distribution'?

"Conditional distribution" means: The distribution of one variable given a specific value of another; found by dividing cell counts by the appropriate row or column total. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Simpson's Paradox'?

"Simpson's Paradox" means: A phenomenon where an association between two variables reverses direction when data are combined across a lurking variable. The other options describe different concepts.
